Here is how TORCH_CHECK_* is defined:
```C++

#include <glog/logging.h>

// Additional macros on top of glog
#ifndef NDEBUG
#define TORCH_CHECK_EQ(val1, val2) CHECK_EQ(val1, val2)
#else // !NDEBUG
// These versions generate no code in optimized mode.
#define TORCH_CHECK_EQ(val1, val2) \
  while (false)                    \
  CHECK_EQ(val1, val2)
#endif // NDEBUG
```


This PR broke FB internals. It seems that the real cause of the problem is the change on the semantics/behaviour of `*CHECK*` on Core. See https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/82032#issuecomment-1203695164.

Regardless, to resolve the situation I'm issuing a forward fix at [D38383266](https://www.internalfb.com/diff/D38383266).

While we're in here, making similar changes, another thing we ought to do rather than split it into two cumbersome schema upgrades is to drop the indices from entries in the resource database. Similar to using names as keys for worlds/areas instead of asset IDs, we should use names as keys for resource types/IDs instead of the numerical indices. `short_name` would be appropriate for this purpose, I think. I imagine it will mostly be a matter of using DictAdapter for them, right? It would be a huge improvement for DB readability, especially when editing templates (which we don't have a UI for yet).

Also, atm the resource db entries have an `extra` field as well which should probably be standardized as a dict as well, for consistency.

When `strict := False`, underscores are be valid in all labels, in any position, even first position, according to the chromium/djb code path.

I can see how what you've implemented suits a case different from the chromium/djb code: For domains used for web browsing, the CAs are [not supposed to](https://github.com/ypcrts/fqdn/issues/14#issuecomment-688604160) sign certs that have an underscore in the apex domain name or public suffix (i.e. TLD).  TLS is so important in 2020 that I see the CAs agreement as almost defining what a valid domain name is now, but originally your case for this option was based off what browsers accept, not what CAs accept. As for what is valid in the real world, I think Chromium source is a good authority. Is there another conflicting authority?

If you have a use case for an option that determines whether underscores are allowed as any of the first char of a label or in the apex domain or TLD, then I'd like to see an authority for that.

I would prefer that we simply match the chromium/djb path with this option.  If you want to make that change, I'll accept it. And like you said in #14, the name `allow_underscores` suits this better, but I can port it over after merge.

Thanks for your work on this again

I noticed that LLVM/clang can emit multiple sections with the same name in order to save space in the resulting binary when -ffunction-sections is used. The current code also doesn't handle this case, so using get_section_by_name() on such an object file will always return the last section with the non-unique name. See the following example:

```
$ cat two_f.c
void a(){}
void b(){}

$ clang-9 -ffunction-sections -fno-unique-section-names -c -o two_f.o two_f.c

$ readelf -S two_f.o
There are 11 section headers, starting at offset 0x208:

Section Headers:
  [Nr] Name              Type             Address           Offset
       Size              EntSize          Flags  Link  Info  Align
  [ 0]                   NULL             0000000000000000  00000000
       0000000000000000  0000000000000000           0     0     0
  [ 1] .strtab           STRTAB           0000000000000000  000001b0
       0000000000000057  0000000000000000           0     0     1
  [ 2] .text             PROGBITS         0000000000000000  00000040
       0000000000000000  0000000000000000  AX       0     0     4
  [ 3] .text             PROGBITS         0000000000000000  00000040
       0000000000000006  0000000000000000  AX       0     0     16
  [ 4] .text             PROGBITS         0000000000000000  00000050
       0000000000000006  0000000000000000  AX       0     0     16
[...]

$ python3
Python 3.6.9 (default, Jul 17 2020, 12:50:27)
[GCC 8.4.0] on linux
>>> from elftools.elf.elffile import ELFFile
>>> elf = ELFFile(open('two_f.o', 'rb'))
>>> print(elf.get_section_by_name('.text').header)
Container({'sh_name': 1, 'sh_type': 'SHT_PROGBITS', 'sh_flags': 6, 'sh_addr': 0, 'sh_offset': 80, 'sh_size': 6, 'sh_link': 0, 'sh_info': 0, 'sh_addralign': 16, 'sh_entsize': 0})
```

In the same manner, your code will always return the last index (in our example above 4) for '.text'.

I'm not sure if that's something that needs general fixing but I would like to keep that in mind.

Additionally, it would be nice to have some test files for your code, be it with non-unique section names or not.

First of all, so exciting to see you back around here @dmontagu! 🤩🎉

## PR Review

Now to the PR review:

I've been reviewing this for the last couple of days. Very clever all around. Amazing job, as always! 🚀

I added a couple of comments here and there. A couple of opinions in the places where I suspect my opinion could be relevant.

## Type Checks from VS Code - PyRight

I noticed a couple of errors with VS Code/PyRight in some places, I think mainly about getting a dict key form a dict where it is not required, or things like that.

Would it be valuable to point them out and suggest the fixes or is it just too much noise on top of the actual PR review?

I'm just adding a couple of those comments for now.

## Questions

About some of the questions in the description, my opinions inline.

Pretty much in favor with everything, for the rest I don't have a strong opinion or enough knowledge.

**Things left to resolve:**

* [ ] Field kwargs
	* [ ] Should we remove or keep the 'examples' kwarg on `Field`?
		* If not, what is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Re-add the examples keyword argument
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. All things equal, +1 to re-adding it. Unless there's something against it.
	* [ ] Should we remove or keep the 'const' kwarg on `Field`? (This was used to indicate a field was required and the only allowed value was its default.)
		* If not, what is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop this argument in favor of `Literal` and/or validators as appropriate (i.e., keep as is on main branch)
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. If it's the same, I would think it's better to keep one single way to do it, and if there's now a "standard" way to do it with type annotations, I would use that. One single way reduces combinatorial logic, maintenance, testing. And it's easier for users to learn one single thing than two, even more if it's two competing ones.
	* [ ] Should we change the `regex` kwarg to `pattern` in the `Field` signature?
		* **Tentative plan:** Change `regex` to `pattern` (i.e., keep as is on main branch)
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. As JSON Schema uses the same `pattern` keyword, I would vote for that. `regex` might be more intuitive from Python-land, but for me, `regex` is more ambiguous as to if it takes a regular expression object or a pattern string. Even though the Python docs also refer to a Pattern object, but anyway, I personally associate the word "pattern" more easily with the string.
	* [ ] Do we want to do a backwards-compatibility handler thing with `**kwargs` — warning about now-invalid kwargs (whether or not we keep them working) on `Field`?
		* **Tentative plan:** Do not attempt to maintain backwards compatibility, but document in the migration guide
			* [@tiangolo] **Strongly** in favor with plan. More details below, and also inline on the PR.


* [ ] ConfigDict fields
	* [ ] Do we want to re-add 'schema_extra' as a field on model_config?
		* If not, should we drop the tests?
		* Either way, do we retain/drop support on a config _class_ in the model definition?
		* What is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop in favor of `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan.
	* [ ] Do we want to re-add 'fields' as a field on model_config?
		* If not, should we drop the tests?
		* What is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop in favor of annotations and/or `= Field(...)`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan.

* [ ] Default values
	* If a default value of a field is not JSON encodable, do we include anything in the schema indicating the default value? Do we error?
	* **Tentative plan:** Just don't include the value in the schema if json encoding fails, but keep the `required` field set appropriately. I believe this is perfectly compatible with JSON schema.
		* [@tiangolo] Soft in favor with plan. I'm not sure about the implications, but I think it should be fine.

* [ ] `__modify_schema__` (-> `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`)
	* Do we retain backwards compatibility with the name `__modify_schema__`, or force migration to `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`?
		* **Tentative plan:** Force the migration to `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan. This is the moment to break changes.
	* Do we support any args/kwargs for `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__` besides the schema itself?
		* Do we pass any info to it from FieldInfo? The CoreSchema? A "source" object? (Could be useful when working with custom generics)
		* **Tentative plan:** Not sure. Would potentially be useful to have access to the core schema and to the type "source" (e.g., to check generic parameter values, etc.)

* [ ] FastAPI
	* Do we need to retain functions analogous to any of the following? (Note we have retained `model_schema`, and `schema`.)
		* get_flat_models_from_field/get_flat_models_from_fields
		* get_flat_models_from_model/get_flat_models_from_models
		* get_model_name_map
		* model_process_schema
	* If not, can we drop their tests?
	* **Tentative plan:** Could we instead build a CoreSchema in FastAPI that produces the desired JSON schema?
		* I'm inclined to put significant effort into helping with the migration of FastAPI to pydantic v2, and to implement any necessary analogous functions as a part of that effort. But if it's already clear that we will need them, happy to add them now.
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan. And thanks, as always! 🎉 Better not to add premature optimizations/abstractions. And more details in line in the PR.

* [ ] Callable type
	* How should we handle JSON schema generation for fields with type callable? (See, e.g., `tests.test_schema.test_callable_type`)
	* **Tentative plan:** Not sure.

* [ ] Decimal
	* What should the Decimal schema be?
	* **Tentative plan:** Need to come to a consensus; https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/1511 may be relevant
		* Note: although serialization and parsing are different, I think for FastAPI/OpenAPI purposes it is important that the JSON schema be compatible with the serialization type. (Or else generated clients will produce type errors if an API returns a Decimal.) Happy to discuss this more.
			* [@tiangolo] agreed that incompatible clients would be a non-obvious but important problem. Although I'm not sure I understand the problem well and what's the "serialization type" in this context.

## kwargs in `Field()`

Details about asking for no more kwargs in `Field()`:

I would request to **not** accept any extra kwargs. I asked for that here in this hidden comment: https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/4516#discussion_r1010870226

Copying here for context/completeness:

> This `**extra` has been used to add stuff to JSON Schema.

> But in some cases, people pass other stuff here expecting it to be used later or for other purposes, but then it also affects JSON Schema.

> I would like to ask to make it a single keyword argument `extra` instead of `**extra`, or alternatively, to have another single argument `json_schema_extra` so that extensions for JSON Schema can be apart from extra values passed here for other purposes.

> I haven't seen a use for this apart from JSON Schema, and the fact that it makes `Field()` accept any keyword arguments makes editors not complain about arguments that actually do nothing. I've seen this sometimes, e.g. recently with a `Field(required=True)`, but `required` is not a valid argument, if it's required or not is defined by the absence of a `default`. But the code seemed fine and linters passed, because it was taken by the catch all `**extra`.

> It's a breaking change, but I think it's totally worth it and this is the right time for those changes.

## Semi-off topic, highlights

**Note**: no action item here, just praise and highlights of non-obvious extra benefits. 🎉

Very clever implementation! 🚀

I like how the core schema refs, def refs, and json refs are stored and correlated, and then updated when necessary, but still stored. And how everything is stored in the object.

I think this will allow me to do something I've been wanting to do in FastAPI, to pre-generate the JSON Schema stuff for each endpoint, and when there's an error, to show the specific endpoint with an error instead of just saying "something is wrong".

## Wishlist - collect errors

I was checking the wishlist of things I wanted for Pydantic and almost everything is already done. 🎉

From the things not there yet, there's one single thing I would like to ask that is related to JSON Schema, although not sure if it could be a subsequent PR. It's also related to the point above.

### The problem

First the problem.

Currently, an example like this, rightfully breaks:

```Pyhton
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict


class Car:
    def __init__(self, make: str, model: str, year: int):
        self.make = make
        self.model = model
        self.year = year

class Model(BaseModel):
    f1: int = 1
    f2: Car

    model_config = ConfigDict(arbitrary_types_allowed=True)


Model.model_json_schema()
```

But if `Model` is in a FastAPI path operation, and maybe `Car` is in a sub-model in a sub-field, in a large app, when calling the OpenAPI endpoint the whole thing will break, saying there's a problem with `Car`, but without saying where exactly... and more importantly, none of the OpenAPI will be generated. So, a single broken field breaks the whole OpenAPI and Swagger UI.

### Feature Request

Currently it immediately raises an error like:

```
Cannot generate a JsonSchema for X
```

...that stops the rest of the JSON Schema generation.

I would like instead to accumulate those errors and get them all together, but to be able to retrieve the rest of the JSON Schema.

### Possible Implementation

I think it's better to state the problem in case you see a better way to solve it, but I can also imagine possible implementations. For example:

```Python
schema = Model.model_json_schema()
```

...raises as normally, but:

```Python
schema, errors = Model.model_json_schema(keep_errors=True)
```

Doesn't raise, and returns a JSON Schema where anything with an error is replaced with `{}` and `errors` is a list of stored exceptions. Of course, `keep_errors` could use some bikeshedding on the parameter name, the same for the return interface, but you get the idea.

It could also be a context manager, maybe too complex, but that would keep the API interface (params, return) of `model_json_schema()` unmodified for both cases, don't know. And it would probably only be used by FastAPI and libraries like that, so not too bad on that side. But it would require contextvars and all that dance. E.g.

```Python
with ErrorCollector as c:
	schema = Model.model_json_schema()
print(e.errors)
```

Again, the specific implementation is up for discussion, but I would like to have a way to do this.

### Plan in FastAPI

My plan is also later to put this in FastAPI in a way that (if I manage to) when there's an error like this, the traceback would use Rich and point to the right file and line with the specific FastAPI *path operation* that uses that model. But I think that to be able to do that I first need to pre-compute and collect the JSON Schema errors per path-operation, and not only at the global level.

## Breaking Changes

About the breaking changes, mostly renaming things, in the small few places this happens, I think this is the right moment to do it and discard the cruft of backwards compatibility for those things.

An improvement in that migration, alternative to keep backwards compatibility, would be a tool to automatically update code to use the new things, for the simple ones (I can imagine, e.g. moving kwargs in `Field()` to `json_schema_extra`, or changing `regex` to `pattern`). Maybe that could also work for the `model_config`.

I haven't done it, but I know it's possible and feasible with LibCST. @Kludex has built a couple of CLI tools that do that type of thing, e.g. https://github.com/Kludex/bump-testclient

Sorry that I've been sitting on this change for a while, I had to find some time to mull it over.

This looks like a harmless change, but my problem with it is that, now, applying and unapplying a patch leaves the object in a different state than it was. consider this example:

```python
class Foobar:
    pass


def mypatch():
    return "foobar"


def main():
    obj = Foobar()

    try:
        obj.printer()
    except AttributeError:
        # expected
        pass

    with Patch(obj, "printer", mypatch):
        print(obj.printer())  # prints "foobar" as expected

    # this now raises a type error: TypeError: 'NoneType' object is not callable
    obj.printer()
```

i don't think this is clean behavior.

i see two possibilities:
* accept that Patch only works for properties that are already there (which is the way it works now, and I think is simple and predictable), and find a different solution for why patch is called with a non-existing attribute in the first place.
* if we must support attributes that don't exist, i suggest replacing `None` with a sentinel object, and when we unapply the patch and the old value is the sentinel object, instead of setting it to the old value, simply delete the attribute. this would then change the above code to raise the `AttributeError` again.

any thoughts?

This was slightly intentional, although obviously not documented very well. SHA256 hashing is not the absolute fastest thing, and so doing it in parallel is a good idea. Doing it inside of `store_bytes` meant that it was running on a `tokio` blocking thread (which there are [more of](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/8935264eea79ffd8cbd40b7067e4f2216a1aae28/src/python/pants/option/global_options.py#L1826-L1833)), rather than on what we call a "core" thread (the threads that `tokio` runs async code on).

So... you might want to benchmark this with the default value of https://www.pantsbuild.org/v2.15/docs/reference-global#rule_threads_core, and with 2x the default?

Hi @tgbugs - thanks for the PR, but I'm not entirely sure I understand the reasoning, though there may be some nuance that I'm missing.

From [Built-in Types — Mapping Types — dict](https://docs.python.org/3.7/library/stdtypes.html#dict)
> **iter(d)**
> Return an iterator over the keys of the dictionary. This is a shortcut for `iter(d.keys())`.

From [Built-in Types — Dictionary view objects](https://docs.python.org/3.7/library/stdtypes.html#dictionary-view-objects)

> The objects returned by [dict.keys()](https://docs.python.org/3.7/library/stdtypes.html#dict.keys), [dict.values()](https://docs.python.org/3.7/library/stdtypes.html#dict.values) and [dict.items()](https://docs.python.org/3.7/library/stdtypes.html#dict.items) are view objects. They provide a dynamic view on the dictionary’s entries, which means that when the dictionary changes, the view reflects these changes.

There may be nuance somewhere here that I'm missing though.

The change is probably fine in that under my interpretation it should have no impact, but I would rather not merge it unless I'm clearer on why it will help.

I think ultimately what is happening with data strucutres being modified in operations that really should only be reading data should be eliminated.

I appreciate the PR and ended up using it myself to test a server in another region.  However there are a few changes I'd suggest.

The current PR puts most of `get_servers()` under a conditional (`if servers_json is None:`), which can possibly break other functionality and makes the diff harder to analyze since things are nested a step further.

I would suggest:

- Change the command line flag to something simpler like just `--json [file.json]`
- Instead of --json overriding get_servers(), have it add the server to the servers list. Instead of the conditional, rearrange it like:
```
#start of get_servers()
"""If it exists, load in a server config from a JSON file."""
if servers_json:
    printer('Loading Servers from:\n%s' % servers_json, debug=True)
    try:
        with open(servers_json) as json_file:
            self.servers = json.load(json_file)
    except FileNotFoundError:
        #handle the exception
#continue with get_servers()
if servers is None:
    #...
```
Then the user can still specify the server via `--server [ID]`.  The reason I suggest this over the current implementation is that there may be cases where the user wants to include the server or servers via JSON but not exclude other servers from being considered relative to "best server based on ping".

Thanks!  Nice work finding out where to make these changes.  This is all good practice at making pull requests :)

I know this is a pretty straightforward change, since it's only to documentation, but attention to detail is still important!  These are small details, but they'll need to get fixed up before merging.

One important detail: when making a pull request, you [always want to start by making a new branch](https://guides.github.com/introduction/flow/).  This PR is made from the `main` branch of your repo, which will make it hard to do other work in your repo, since `main` should always be the latest version of the main repository.

One last point about the pull request -- the title should describe very briefly what it does.  In this case, that might be "Document that index paths cannot contain slashes".  That way when we're looking through long lists of PRs, we can know quickly what this one is.  "Documentation add" doesn't mean much, and "Issue #3721" is only helpful if we remember what that issue number means.  It's usually good to add a bit longer explanation in the PR description: why this change is made, and where the documentation will appear.  And if you end the description with "Fixes #3721" then GitHub will automatically link the issue and the PR, and will even close the issue when the PR is merged.

Unfortunately, it's not possible to change the branch from which a pull request is made, so we'll need to close this one up.  Next steps:
 * create a new branch in your repo (I usually name mine after the issue, e.g., `issue3721`)
 * make the changes and commit them (think of a good commit message!)
 * make a new PR

If you're still working on getting your environment set up, then we can run `yarn generate` for you and push the changes to your branch.  I can't push changes to your `main` branch, so I can't do it for you on this PR.

  * `_tools/gen_exports.py`
  * `_tests/_check_type_completeness.py` or at least `pyright --verifytypes` (on all platforms) once that passes
  * `pip-compile -q ...` ... except good luck doing that without `tox` to manage python version, but there's discussion in #2681 and there's #2595

* I personally also have `autoflake` enabled

This requires `urllib3>=1.26.0` ([relevant commit](https://github.com/urllib3/urllib3/commit/382ab32f23795c44faae83b4e8b18a16fb605a0a)). However, we don't currently list `urllib3` as a dependency at all in `setup.py` (and `pex-requirements.txt`), which makes sense because that package isn't imported/used directly. `requests` depends on `urllib3>=1.21.1,<1.27`; the minimum version listed in our `setup.py` doesn't even properly depend on it because it was vendored at the time...

In other words, this change, without modifications to `setup.py`, would brick installations with old versions of `requests` or `urllib3` where only `internetarchive` gets upgraded since `pip` would think that no other changes are necessary. Likely there'd also be no obvious indication of what's wrong, i.e. that those packages need to be upgraded.

I guess adding `urllib3>=1.26.0` as an explicit dependency in `setup.py` is the least messy way out of that. Modern `pip` versions should then realise whether `requests` needs to be upgraded as well on a `pip install --upgrade internetarchive` to satisfy that requirement. Might be good to test this though.
A bump of the version requirement for `requests` may also be necessary to handle installations with ancient versions of `requests` that still vendored `urllib3`; that would be `requests` version 2.16.0 or newer. A possibly better one would be to bump it to the first version that supported `urllib3==1.26.0`, and that's 2.25.0 (even Debian stable has that...).

This is not a hypothetical scenario, by the way. Trying to prevent another #377.

I worrying about only one thing. After this PR port `12400` is treated as "not set". This is not true from the user point of view. Imagine the following scenario. One reads LightGBM docs and finds that port for distributed training can be set via [`local_listen_port`](https://lightgbm.readthedocs.io/en/latest/Parameters.html#local_listen_port) parameter and its default value is `12400`. User doesn't have any preferences for ports and decides to leave default value (or more critical problem: harcodes `12400` in params). The user goes to firewall setting and opens `12400` port with the hope that everything will work. But it won't work because actually **random**, not `12400`, port will be used inside LightGBM.

Things are getting worse due to that there is no dedicated `port` param in Dask wrapper that can be set to say `None` and take priority over `local_listen_port` is kwargs. I have only one solution for this inconsistency for now:
document in Dask docs that actually **only for Dask** port `12400 === random`.

I'm not sure changing the dictionary order is a good way to handle this. While it will work and it's fine in CPython, pure python itself doesn't guarantee the order of a dictionary, dicts are supposed to be an inherently unordered data type, and in other implementations of python, such as Pypy, iterating through the dict may result in a different order than the one in which the dict was defined.

Even in CPython it's not guaranteed that this current ordered behavior of dicts will be preserved in future versions. Instead, it may be wise to use `collections.OrderedDict`, or an approach with 2 separate dictionaries (like `STYLE_MAP` and `COLOR_MAP`), checking the first one before the other one. In fact, in previous CPython versions, this was not the case and dict order was essentially random.

abravalheri is correct. The script wrappers / launcher supplied by Setuptools is deprecated and would be removed except that it's relied upon by editable-installed packages (`pip install -e`).

I'd prefer to see these custom-built wrappers obviated entirely by pip-installed wrappers or replace these wrappers with distlib-backed launchers.

I'd be okay with this PR except that it's expanding the duplication found in the build scripts and adding more complex logic to them (creating directories, deleting existing directories, changing directories). If we're going to accept any more complexity to the scripts, I'd like to see them ported to Python and consolidated (avoiding repetition).

Before accepting, I'll want the binaries to be generated by one of the maintainers, so they're committed/signed by a maintainer. 

abravalheri suggested adding test(s). Tests would be nice to capture the failure and prevent a regression. I'd be okay to skip tests given that this functionality is deprecated anyway.

@prabhat00155 All the question can be answered with "Because we are using a static analyzer on dynamic code". Usually when we encounter weirdness, it is either because `mypy` is unnecessarily strict or we actually have spaghetti / unsafe code that should be refactored. Given that the latter is usually not obvious, we opted to simply silence `mypy` where it complains, since the code works as is.

Tentatively approving.. the expr to generate the new funcall heuristic looks good. Just the state machine formulation breaks my brain and I'm not sure it hits exactly the intended locations for the rewrite, which is also complicated by the fact that I'm not sure which intended locations we're trying to hit.

It seems that:

- we want to limit rewrite to expressions that are directly or indirectly contribute to constructing args for an Invoke (that is not an Output-form Invoke). The *indirectly* part is net new  in the PR, hence new recursion with postvisitor != nil that was not there before

- we want to find __convert(ObjectConsExpression(..), destTy) and rewrite them.

This seems to do that. The new example code illustrates the "indirectly" part - the `sid = "1"` is part of an array expr that contributes to statements property, not a direct property of the invoke args.

```
policyDocument = invoke("aws:iam:getPolicyDocument", {
  statements = [{
    sid = "1"

    actions = [
      "s3:ListAllMyBuckets",
      "s3:GetBucketLocation",
    ]

    resources = [
      "arn:aws:s3:::*",
    ]
  }]
})

resource example "aws:iam:Policy" {
  name   = "example_policy"
  path   = "/"
  policy = policyDocument.json
}
```

So while the previous code that used recursion was easier for me to read I bet it was not reaching deep enough into the tree to fix that expr up.

I tried rewriting in `t0yv0/8821` using F#-inspired pattern recognizers but that didn't pass this test case as the intention here is to rewrite deeply it seems; the state machine fits the problem better in the sense of what the VisitExpression interface coerces.

Now... What I'm left wondering is why the optional fixup is limited to invoke sub-expression only (that distinction seems a bit fragile). I was wondering the same before this PR and generally as I recall we can't trust the expression trees to be well-typed between passes, and the current pass sequence / obligations are not yet? documented.. So that's something we need to work on .. But approving the PR.
